item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since When Day Breaks invented the concept of "narrator reading something on a computer", I must no-vote for plagiarism. Please be more original next time.

(no, seriously, this is great, upvote)

EDIT : I just remembered this stupid argument has been critized in a short comedy film right here. There are (not very good) english subs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly my thought. Sleep is just a state of chemically induced unconsciousness. - Yes, the chemicals are produced by your own body, but they are not fundamentally different from ones we can produce externally.

The only way I can figure it is that the SCP considers those chemicals a part of 'you' and the external ones not. Obviously this SCP has a way to determine what is or is not you, since it maintains 'you' even when your brain has been put through a blender, so that works.

Still, I think that only being able to fall asleep 'naturally' is definitely a strange enough aspect of the anomaly to warrant being mentioned in the documentation. - Though then again, maybe it was, the official document is cutoff when D-11424 stops reading it, so they could have mentioned it during that part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t see how this is much like When Day Breaks.

The only similarities are that it’s told from the perspective of someone on a computer and that the person is taken away at the end.

item.title: memetica I guess
item.content:encoded: what would happen if after Ωk occurred something like SCP-3125 were to breach containment. would it bypass the end of death by killing things in the noshpere leaving behind a living but innactive brain or would it just cause insanity. or would it be neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why the mix between scp and tale? Is this a new trend the community has embraced that I'm not aware of? A less strict definition of what an SCP entry is supposed to be? I'm not being sarcastic. I did enjoy it a lot. I'm just confused. Are there more skips like this that I haven't seen?

I'd upvote for the content and downvote for the format. I'm kinda leaning towards the latter. But I feel it'd be unfair to do so without having more context. Help, anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could be wrong, because it's been months since I read these, but I'm fairly certain the D-11424 in this canon is the girl one (Jamie) from Numbers Never Die and not the meat-hole-wandering, pirate-hat-clad one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real question is.
Did D-11424 get to wear his pirate hat when he was beheaded?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What makes everything more horrific is that as we see, even venues that could help those in unrecoverable conditions via final death(The knife) or provide relief(Via storing minds inside on particular robot) are discarded due to goalpost shifting, cover-ups and fanatic clinging to Foundation views

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good article, some minor corrections:

"Note that D-8833 remained conscious despite [not having] a significant volume of blood."
Somewhat irregular phrasing, maybe "missing" would work better in [].

"Subject sustained major head injury [mark] was admitted to Site-06 medical ward with severe brain damage and blood loss."
Missing "and" or similar at [mark]

"Electrical signals persisted throughout the brain cells suspended in solution as would be expected for a healthy human brain, leading to the conclusion that [D-390A] affects the individual cells of the brain itself."
"D-390A" in [] should probably be "SCP-3984"

"The amount of function is not retained so long as the brain itself remains operable (see Experiment 03)."
Hard to parse, does it mean that the amount of structure the brain retains is not directly correlated with how much function it retains? i.e. there seems to be a lower bound below which function does not drop no matter what is done to the brain?

+1 The experiments were very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having a real hard time getting past Torchwood's Miracle Day in my head - and I didn't much care for Miracle Day.

But …Ultimately I do. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there's a monopoly on being able to do prose in SCPs like this, but I don't think it's particularly well-done here. The D-Class in question is pretty interesting, but I don't really feel attached at any point aside from said interest. The revealing of information through each log is pretty good, but you cut away at the final point of the story from the real interest. I get it's there to set up for future stories, but I think this is mostly bad form if you're gonna tease the audience in such a way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, but that's not what the article specifies. So, are sponges immortal, or not? If they are, how does that reconcile with the whole brain function explanation? If not, fix the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It specifies the Cavalier-Smith system which aims to base things off of common ancestor branchings, so Kingdom Animalia starts at the point where a group of Apoikozoa started making complex colonies. Based on that, I'd guess the minimum bar is the nerve net thing, since the first split inside Animalia is between Porifera and animals that aren't sponges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, first extremely pedantic question I had was, what about archaea and non-algae protists?

Then, upon taking a nap, realized the much bigger issue: what about all the animals without brains, or even nervous systems???

If it affects only brain function, you need to exclude the remaining unicellular organisms, and probably at least Porifera (though, apparently, their undifferentiated cells may communicate somehow, so Porifera could instead be 100% immortal I guess, the whole thing classified as a brain?) Cnidaria only have nerve nets, still nothing that is specifically a brain. After that, there are at least nerve cords with something resembling a brain at the front.

item.title: lmao
item.content:encoded: nice, well done lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was pretty interesting. I'm gonna bet that Dr Young was instructed NOT to revert the Omega K scenario because of what happened with 05-11 and Miriam in SCP-2718. Maybe 05-1 never forgot? Maybe they reviewed the files. Can't wait to see the next part. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of Torchwood: Miracle Day…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So not only have you looked at the piece as a line-by-line, but you've looked into the plot in depth, identified most if not all of the questions I left unanswered and most if not all of then intentional "problems" (as well as a good many unintentional ones). And it's not even all negative, there's praise mixed in as well. This is an excellent review.

+ show block
– hide block

Concerns about Young - all of which are valid - will hopefully be addressed later in the series. But she is a monster.

The purpose of the research on 3984 isn't to establish an alternative to death in the containment sense. Sure, you can just throw someone in a cell and leave them there and no one needs to know, but they'll be in that cell forever, alive. What about the researchers on this project? They don't want that to happen to themselves in the end.

The motivation of the narrator is to discover what caused the immortality. They don't find any information on that in this article, because Young has restricted research (that's why the narrator specifically comments on that after the ConProcs)

Per the rules of the contest I can't significantly change the content of the article, but I can correct some of the errors you've pointed out and will be doing so later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, sorry, thanks for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can we get a collapsible here? :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ. Immortality and you decide to murder other people in the most horrific ways possible? Knowing how to kill people is a niche thing the foundation has alternative methods to get the same result with already, like amnestics and just keeping the damn person they don't want in action locked up.

Review below.

+ show block
– hide block

The narrator, I think, is a type that overexplains jokes.
[Security cameras, their gaze trained upon the door through which you just entered, are blind. You cut their power half an hour ago. You can never be too safe.]
[You know that it won’t creak when you open it because one of the maintenance workers oiled it a week ago. That maintenance worker was you. You don’t work here. The door swings open smoothly without so much as a groan.]
[You arrive at the librarians’ desk and, somewhat awkwardly, climb over the counter. Behind, you take a seat at one of the two computers and boot it up. The fans of the machine turn on, blowing their cacophony into the silent room. After twenty seconds — this is outdated technology, after all — you are presented with a login screen. You withdraw a notebook from your pocket and enter the details on the first page. The login is successful.]
What would have been a snappy bit flops. The narration can't decide whether it wants to mechanically describe everything or to have a tension-filled break in. Italics are sentences that aren't necessary, but would add detail or personality in another tale, but emphasizing the boring mechanicalness of the narrator where what happened does so more explicitly and effectively isn't subtle or interesting.

[You don’t need to rush. You have all the time in the world.]
This is really weird to say given all the focus on carefulness earlier.

[The file for SCP-3984 lights up the room as the page loads, dark red header contrasting against the bright white background, burning your eyes that have grown used to the darkness. You wince, and blink, and wait for your eyes to adjust.]
Something about the narrator visiting the same site we use gets me. Ow.

[You are professionals — act like it. It’s not like this is going to kill you.]
But they don't know that. The entire point of these experiments is to figure out a way they might die. Plus, uh, torture.
[Prohibited? That doesn’t make sense. Why would the Foundation want to actively restrict research?]
This seems like a cog that can easily be lost in the bureaucracy of Foundation, someone above Young ordered it, antimemetics or something dangerous or deadend to study, heck, self-preservation because not-death is a Good Thing to friends or family near it. What makes it more interesting is why the narrator focuses on it when Foundation has plenty of reasons for restricting it. Immortality has been a Bad Thing to society throughout and it shows how Foundation is insane to ignore it? Immortality's been torture to them, suspicions Young continuing this to get her torture-jollies off? The second is really shitty, in other terms: for someone to be tortured badly enough to want death.

[D-1190 was asphyxiated by manual strangulation from D-9981.]
The worst part of this is that it starts off making sense. Okay, you decide the antibaby gas won't be enough and make the leap to SO LET'S KILL PEOPLE INSTEAD but that's just in case we can't find something else :), okay. Figuring out how to kill humans before the [much larger short-term concern] of killing animals is backwards priorities, but okay, maybe there's some people Foundation needs to kill and can't contain or amnestize or anything else.

[For example, does it prevent aging and injury, is there a healing factor, does it simply remove an assailant's ability to kill?]
These kinds of unknowns means that the ethic-committeeless Foundation really does need to try things like forcing one prisoner to strangle another! Let's keep in with the theme of going with backwards priorities and do the most trauma-inducing and slow-horrible ways to die first, and then send the subjects in again and again for re-exposure to the same trauma and brain damage.

[Note that D-8833 remained conscious despite not having a significant volume of blood.]
The usual question is, is it worth it? Except that the 'worth it' in this scenario is to be able to kill more people in horrible ways. Young is doing things that are blatantly cruel and unnecessary for a very conditional and questionably useful cause. Young is a monster.

[The logs continue in much the same fashion, with some unfortunate D-Class being exposed to something that would normally kill them. Poison, starvation, explosion, whatever. You scroll down, looking for one in particular. The old scrollwheel clicks loudly, twenty times with each movement of your finger, a series of loud clicks that echo through the silence of the library. You stop at Experiment 10.]
This is good. The causal glanceover of [Poison, starvation, explosion, whatever] really shows the extent of the torture Young committed, and how something so awful has become commonplace.

[You smile, lifting your hand to touch the raised scar that forms a ring around your neck and the tiny bumps from age-old stitches. Irreparable.]
Absolutely horrifying, twistedly succinct, perfect. That the narrator was a test subject makes this so much better.

[Subject physically recovered within three days but remained in a vegetative state for several weeks. Upon awakening, D-1190 displayed symptoms of permanently-impaired motor and speech skills as well as widespread paralysis.]
If this was about taking out someone who needed to be killed when amnestics and containment and literally everything else couldn't work, then here is where experimenting should have stopped. Here's your way to stop them, make them a vegetable, there won't be a person to feel it if you let them starve.
But it's not, it keeps going, and what reason left is there to justify this?

[Dr. Young has neglected to maintain a log of specific research intentions for each experiment. ]
Apparently lack of organization and letting people shrug and go 'well I want to test this' whenever they want. As much as the Foundation likes to log things and go with logic, bureaucracy has apparently set its fingers in, in a place that really suffers with the incompetency and loopholes bureaucracy brings.

[D-374A, a female Macaca mulatta or Rhesus macaque]
FINALLY SOMEONE TESTS AN ANIMAL INSTEAD OF GOING STRAIGHT FOR HUMANS. Seriously, starting with humans is like saying, okay we found this new chemical, let's inject it in people instead of the mice that were bred for this purpose. Especially since immortality in this case affected everybody and everything, including animals.
Oh, I guess if you want another pointless reason that doesn't make for any of this, the meat industry relies on the death of animals.
Keeping in line, this is bad science. You test more than one animal in case one naturally resists a chemical, the doser malfunctions, etc. As a fix, replace the subject with like [Twenty Macaca mulatta or Rhesus macaques]. You could emphasize the rigor with like [the average mass-corrected response wasn't significantly different] for the sedatives that work, but I'd suggest against it if you don't know what those terms mean (they have specific scientific meanings that people co-opt which makes them even worse, like how theorize was lost as meaning the same things as hypothesize). I think the baseline for rigor in scps is low enough that these logs don't show anything special about the researcher for most people. (Maybe I'll make a gen community post about this.)

[You've seen enough test logs. None of them contain the information you're looking for. You know that at some point there'll be one where they reconnect D-11424's severed head, but beyond that, none of them interest you.]
Aw, narrator! I'm not sure if you intend apathy or disgust by [none of them interest you] but it sounds like apathy, which is a little incongruous with [If it explains why Young was the lead researcher for this, why she spent so long testing on humans without rhyme or reason, why research into ΩK is forbidden… then you need to know about it.]. All in all I'm kind of confused on the narrator's motivation: I guess wanting to know why they cut your head off is a reason to want to know why, but I talked about why I thought specifically thinking a research ban on ΩK is weird is a weird thing to think, maybe add more clarification around here?

The last log is really nice, by the way. The oh-shit danger moment and sequence after that is gripping.

Typos:
[Since that time, SCP-3984 has been present in every living creature thus tested in the.]
[She will undergo psychological pending on her recovery.]
Dropped a word here.
[Research efforts must be directed into the development of alternative methods of simulating death.]
Simulating means to do something in a fake way, which probably isn't what you're looking for here. 'Inducing', perhaps.
[D-373A was given a standard lethal injection dose, i.e. was injected with the following substances with 3-minute gaps between injections:]
Should be better as a footnote, because if you say 'standard lethal injection dose' then it's standard enough for the readers to know what that means and not have to be introduced to it in the main body of text. The problem from a writing standpoint it's inconsistent, putting r-selected as a footnote but not lethal injection chemicals.
[3. For example, does it prevent aging and injury, is there a healing factor, does it simply remove an assailant's ability to kill?]
For consistency's sake I would recommend putting this (analysis, not scientific fact) in the main body as well, and because I read this, looked for it again in the paragraph ten minutes, and then realized it was a footnote.
[D-374A]
Nonhuman entities wouldn't be labeled D-class because they're not personnel.


Please collapse long posts- Mgmt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was horrific. It was almost hard to get through; I was cringing during the middle section of the scip. It's so dark, Team Bird's articles are practically a cartoon in comparison.

+1 nicely done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also got Elantris vibes from this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lmao

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The internal thoughts of the researcher that you are supposed to be in the mind of really brings a sense of personality to this article.

Amazing job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the inability to become unconscious render sleep unobtainable? I would imagine that would be the more immediate issue. Maybe I just missed something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hello, death?"
"Death broke."
"Understandable have a nice day."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the possible titles for this piece was going to be When Death Breaks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the comparisons to When Day Breaks coming in I want to say that I actually like this one more. The format break is a lot more reigned in and the actual skips stands on its own rather than the entire thing being so obtuse as to be more of a tale than a skip. I also have an easier time investing myself in this one because of the characters.

edit Although I’ve grown fonder of When Day Breaks over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Okay. In the Clutches of Life finally makes sense.

That said, my reaction to this is that it's an interesting concept, but the format adds nothing, and I've seen it before. When Day Breaks, as previously mentioned. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammerung?
I’m interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with CWW's sentiments on why they didn't try to chemically destroy the brain (unless that is going to prop up in a later tale), but I felt that the testing logs provided some solid horror, especially with Experiment Log 24 (the possibility of still being alive in that state disturbs me a lot). The mentions of Dammerung serve as a nice hook for later stuff as well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a fan of this one. Super unsettling and it reminds me of the novel “Elantris” by Brandon Sanders (which I am currently readin). My question is, if the anomoly makes it so you cannot lose consciousness, how do people fall asleep? Or sleeping not the same as being knocked out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote. While it was fairly entertaining, and the Dammerung connection is interesting, it's almost a beat-for-beat reconstruction of When Day Breaks, and I don't know how I feel about that.

Also, why didn't they try totally destroying anything on a chemical level? Surely by like, throwing a rat in the incinerator, you'd force something interesting to happen — a way to halt life for real, or brain matter that can't be burnt to a crisp, or a brain that can be burnt to a crisp and still work.

item.title: E
item.content:encoded: Very good. Looking forward to finding out what’s behind that Level 5 lock.

item.title: Author Post · Poking Death with a Stick
item.content:encoded:
The second entry to the Doomsday Contest by team I don't wanna die.

When the Foundation finds out that people can't die, what's the first thing they do? Try to kill people, of course!

Many thanks to my teammates Captain Kirby, OthellotheCat, Veiedhimaedhr for their support and criticism, and the same to Ayers!

I hope you enjoy.

More stuff by me
